narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 55% chance of not passing. Picture your journey like a story the model pieced together from key chapters of your academic life, and the one that loomed largest was your Year 3 performance, where you ranked in the bottom decile (1.0) at your school—that low standing whispered the strongest warning of struggles ahead on the bar. Building on that, your Year 1 decile of 4.0 didn't shine as brightly as it could have, pulling the narrative further toward challenges in keeping up with rigorous legal demands. Your LSAT score of 30 added to the concern, signaling to the model that the intense analytical hurdles of bar prep might prove tough. Even with your high family income, which often opens doors, it strangely aligned in this story with patterns of past students who still faced bar pitfalls. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 layered in another thread of steady-but-not-soaring academics, while being a white male in a full-time program fit profiles the model has seen falter before. It's not that any one piece defined you, but together, they painted a picture of someone whose academic trajectory—especially that sharp drop from Year 1 to Year 3—suggested the bar's high-stakes grind could be an uphill battle. This isn't the end of your story, though; many have rewritten similar scripts by targeting those weaker spots with focused prep. You've got strengths like your full-time commitment that could turn the tide—what if you aimed to boost that LSAT or rebuild your decile rankings in targeted practice? We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong and pass that bar with flying colors. Keep pushing forward.
